source.title: Network-level encoding of local neurotransmitters in cortical astrocytes

confidence_rationale: Directly demonstrates that brief local neurotransmitter inputs produce long-lasting astrocyte Ca responses over a minutes-long time course. This quantitatively constrains tau_R to be orders of magnitude longer than single synaptic events (milliseconds) and E1's prediction horizon (seconds to tens of seconds), placing tau_R in the minutes range for the network-level Ca component.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Local, transient neurotransmitter inputs are encoded by broad cortical astrocyte networks over a minutes-long time course, contributing to evidence that substantial astrocyte-neuron communication occurs across slow, network-level spatiotemporal scales.
mapping.ree_translation: tau_R -- the time constant of R field decay in REE -- must be calibrated to the temporal scale at which astrocyte Ca responses persist. This paper establishes that the network-level Ca response to a brief input lasts on the order of minutes. Since E1 operates at a prediction horizon of 20 steps and E2 at a rollout horizon of 30 steps (likely seconds), tau_R should be set in the range of 1-10 minutes for the slow network Ca component -- a factor of 10-100 longer than E1/E2 timescales. This ensures R(x,t) retains regulatory history across multiple E1/E2 cycles.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The 'minutes-long' time course refers to network-level Ca responses, not individual cell or microdomain responses which are shorter. REE's tau_R should probably be a composite of multiple timescales (fast microdomain: seconds; slow network: minutes). The photouncaging delivery in this study is non-naturalistic; naturalistic synaptic drive patterns may produce different temporal profiles. The rodent cortex context differs from REE's abstract latent space.
mapping.source_context: Mouse cortex, ex vivo and in vivo two-photon astrocyte Ca imaging, photouncaging of GABA and glutamate, Poskanzer lab (UCSF).

confidence_components.notes: Nature publication; direct in vivo temporal characterization of Ca response duration. Mapping fidelity is high because the paper explicitly quantifies the minutes-timescale, directly answering the tau_R order-of-magnitude question. Transfer risk is low conceptually but the exact calibration numbers require context-specific adjustment.
